event: Early modern humans inhabit the region, with the Makgadikgadi basin and surrounding areas supporting hunter-gatherer populations.
Stone-tool-making peoples occupy the area, with later archaeological evidence suggesting ritual activity at Tsodilo Hills among the oldest in the world.
Ancestors of the San (Bushmen) peoples live across the Kalahari as foraging hunter-gatherers, leaving extensive rock-art traditions.
Khoekhoe (Khoi) pastoralists herding cattle and sheep spread into the region, introducing livestock economies alongside San foragers.
Bantu-speaking agropastoralists begin migrating into the area, bringing iron-working, farming, and cattle-keeping.
Iron Age communities establish settlements and engage in regional trade networks linking the interior to the Indian Ocean coast.
The Toutswe state flourishes in eastern Botswana, with large cattle-based chiefdoms centred on hilltop settlements like Toutswemogala.
The decline of Toutswe coincides with the rise of Great Zimbabwe and the shifting of trade and political power in the wider region.
Tswana-speaking (Sotho-Tswana) chiefdoms emerge and consolidate, founding the dynastic lineages that would shape Botswana's politics.
Major Tswana morafe (tribal groups) such as the Bakwena, Bangwaketse, and Bangwato form through fission and expansion across the eastern hardveld.
European traders and explorers begin reaching the region from the south, increasing contact and trade in ivory and skins.
The London Missionary Society begins missionary work among the Tswana, introducing Christianity and literacy.
The Difaqane (Mfecane) upheavals send waves of displaced peoples and raiders through the region, disrupting Tswana communities.
Ndebele raids under Mzilikazi pressure Tswana groups before the Ndebele are pushed north across the Limpopo by the Boers.
Missionary David Livingstone settles among the Bakwena under Chief Sechele and begins his explorations of the interior.
Boer commandos attack Sechele's Bakwena at the Battle of Dimawe during tensions over arms and missionary influence.
Chief Khama III of the Bangwato comes to power and becomes a noted Christian reformer and modernizing ruler.
Britain proclaims the Bechuanaland Protectorate over the Tswana territories to forestall Boer and German expansion during the Scramble for Africa.
Cecil Rhodes's British South Africa Company pushes to absorb Bechuanaland into its commercial empire.
Chiefs Khama III, Bathoen, and Sebele travel to London and persuade the British to keep the Protectorate under Crown control rather than Company rule.
A devastating rinderpest epidemic kills much of the region's cattle, undermining the Tswana economy.
The administrative capital for the Protectorate is established at Mafeking (Mafikeng), located outside the territory in South Africa.
Khama III dies after nearly half a century of rule, leaving a strong legacy among the Bangwato.
Seretse Khama marries Englishwoman Ruth Williams, triggering a political crisis with apartheid South Africa and Britain.
Britain exiles Seretse Khama from the Protectorate under pressure from South Africa over his interracial marriage.
Seretse Khama is allowed to return to Bechuanaland as a private citizen after renouncing his chieftaincy claim.
Early nationalist parties form as African political organization grows across the Protectorate.
Seretse Khama co-founds the Bechuanaland Democratic Party (later the Botswana Democratic Party) to lead the country to independence.
The capital is moved from Mafeking to the newly built city of Gaborone, and self-government begins under Seretse Khama as Prime Minister.
Bechuanaland gains independence as the Republic of Botswana on 30 September, with Seretse Khama as its first President.
Botswana joins the United Nations shortly after independence.
Major diamond deposits are discovered at Orapa, beginning the transformation of Botswana's economy.
The Debswana joint venture between the government and De Beers is established to mine and market the country's diamonds.
The Orapa diamond mine opens, becoming one of the world's largest, and Botswana begins decades of rapid economic growth.
President Seretse Khama dies in office and is succeeded by Vice-President Quett (Ketumile) Masire.
The Jwaneng diamond mine opens, becoming one of the richest diamond mines in the world by value.
South African forces raid Gaborone, attacking alleged ANC targets amid regional tensions over apartheid.
Festus Mogae becomes President as Ketumile Masire steps down after long service.
The government's relocation of San (Basarwa) communities from the Central Kalahari Game Reserve sparks domestic and international controversy.
Botswana's High Court rules that the eviction of San people from the Central Kalahari Game Reserve was unlawful.
Ian Khama, son of Seretse Khama, becomes President after Festus Mogae steps down.
The global financial crisis and falling diamond demand sharply contract Botswana's economy, exposing its reliance on diamonds.
Mokgweetsi Masisi becomes President after Ian Khama completes his term, leading to a later public rift between the two men.
Botswana's High Court decriminalizes same-sex relations, and the ruling BDP wins elections amid the Khama-Masisi split.
The Botswana Democratic Party loses power for the first time since independence as Duma Boko of the UDC wins the presidency.
